name: Kelly Ghost Town
short_description: Historic mining ghost town on BLM land where rockhounds can search for fluorite, calcite, and quartz specimens left behind from silver and lead mining operations. Free public access with a 25 lb da...
description:
Kelly Ghost Town sits in the heart of New Mexico's mining heritage, a preserved settlement that once thrived as a silver and lead mining hub in the 1880s-1890s. The site remains largely intact with abandoned buildings, old mine workings, and scattered mineralized material accessible to rockhounds. The surrounding BLM land preserves both the historical structures and the geological deposits that drew miners here over a century ago.

This location offers a unique combination of history and geology. Visitors can explore the ghost town while searching for specimens in the tailings and surrounding rock outcrops. Fluorite in purple and green varieties, white and clear calcite, quartz crystals, and occasionally silver-bearing specimens can be found in the mineralized zones around the old mining areas.

The terrain is characterized by high desert scrubland with scattered juniper and pinyon pine. The elevation sits around 6,000 feet, providing relief from the intense heat of lower desert areas. The site is accessible year-round, though summer can be hot and winter mornings can be cold at this elevation. The combination of educational value, historical interest, and genuine rockhounding opportunity makes Kelly Ghost Town a worthwhile destination for collectors of all experience levels.

Because this is active BLM land with archaeological and historical significance, collectors must follow regulations carefully. The 25 lb daily limit per FLPMA applies, and no motorized excavation or commercial collection is permitted. Respect for the historic structures and other visitors is essential.
history: The Kelly mining district emerged in the 1880s during New Mexico's silver boom, with rich deposits of silver, lead, and zinc attracting prospectors and miners. The fluorite, calcite, and quartz specimens found here today are byproducts of hydrothermal activity that deposited these minerals in association with the precious metal veins, a process driven by Tertiary-age magmatism across the New Mexico mining belt.
tips: Search the old tailings piles and eroded slopes around the base of the mining structures where mineralized rock has been exposed and weathered over time. Look for purple and green fluorite crystals in association with white calcite in the fractured rock and small prospect pits. Bring a small backpack, hammer, and chisel - the mineralized material requires careful extraction but specimens break cleanly. Visit in early morning or late afternoon to avoid the afternoon heat and to get better lighting for spotting translucent fluorite in the rocks.
terrain_notes: High desert terrain at approximately 6,000 feet elevation with sparse juniper and pinyon pine vegetation. The ground is rocky and uneven with scattered exposed rock outcrops and eroded tailings. Walking distance from parking to active collecting areas is typically 0.25 to 0.5 miles. Footing is rough with loose rocks and uneven ground; sturdy hiking boots are essential. The area is exposed with minimal shade.
best_season: October - April. The high elevation (6,000 ft) makes summers hot and exposed, while the desert location experiences occasional winter snow that can close roads. Fall and spring offer ideal temperatures for exploring the site and searching the tailings without heat stress.
written_directions: From Socorro, head north on NM 25 for approximately 8 miles toward Magdalena. At Magdalena, continue west on US 60 for about 2 miles. Turn south on a marked BLM access road (look for historical signs) and follow it for approximately 2 miles to the Kelly Ghost Town site. The road is rough dirt and suitable for high-clearance vehicles; passenger cars may scrape in places. Park in the informal lot near the old buildings.
road_conditions: Rough dirt road with washboard surface, high-clearance recommended, passenger cars can access with care
parking_notes: Informal dirt parking area near the ghost town buildings. Space for 5-8 vehicles. No facilities.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation. Respect archaeological and historical features - do not collect from or damage historic structures. All specimens must be extracted by hand tools only.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
No collecting from or damaging historic buildings and structures
Hand tools only
nearest_services: Socorro, approximately 12 miles away - full services including gas, food, and water
accessibility_notes: Rough uneven terrain with loose rocks. Not suitable for wheelchair access or limited mobility. Sturdy hiking boots required. The site involves some walking over uneven ground, though the main collecting areas are accessible within short walks from parking.

faq.question: What minerals can you find rockhounding at Kelly Ghost Town in New Mexico?
faq.answer: Kelly Ghost Town is known for fluorite (purple and green varieties), white and clear calcite, quartz crystals, and occasional silver-bearing specimens. These minerals are found in the surrounding rock outcrops and old tailings piles from historic mining operations that extracted silver and lead from this area.

faq.question: Do you need a permit to rockhound at Kelly Ghost Town?
faq.answer: No permit is required for recreational rockhounding at Kelly Ghost Town, which sits on BLM land. However, you must follow BLM regulations: personal use only, maximum 25 lbs per day, no commercial collection, and no motorized excavation.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to go rockhounding near Socorro?
faq.answer: October through April is ideal for Kelly Ghost Town. The high elevation (6,000 feet) makes summers too hot and exposed, while this period offers comfortable temperatures. Winter snow can occasionally close the access road, so check conditions before visiting.

faq.question: Is Kelly Ghost Town rockhounding site beginner-friendly?
faq.answer: Yes, Kelly Ghost Town is excellent for beginners. Fluorite, calcite, and quartz are relatively easy to identify, and specimens can be found on the surface and in weathered tailings without deep excavation. The site combines history and geology, making it educational and engaging for new rockhounders.

faq.question: How far is Kelly Ghost Town from Socorro and what are the road conditions?
faq.answer: Kelly Ghost Town is approximately 12 miles north and west of Socorro. Access requires driving a rough dirt road (about 2 miles from the highway) that is suitable for high-clearance vehicles. Passenger cars can access it carefully, but expect washboard surface and some rough spots.

faq.question: Can you bring dogs and children to Kelly Ghost Town for rockhounding?
faq.answer: Yes, Kelly Ghost Town welcomes dogs and is family-friendly for children ages 6 and up. The site combines ghost town exploration with rockhounding, making it engaging for kids. Keep children and dogs away from old mine openings and unstable structures, and bring plenty of water as the site is exposed and hot in certain seasons.

meta_title: Kelly Ghost Town Rockhounding - New Mexico
meta_description: Hunt for fluorite, calcite, and quartz at Kelly Ghost Town near Socorro on free BLM land. Historic mining ghost town with hand collecting only.
